title: The End of Evangelion A Textual Analysis
display_title: The End of Evangelion A Textual Analysis
wikitext:
[]: The End of Evangelion: A Textual Analysis             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Mar 06, 2013 8:20 pm First off, I’m not here to discuss the mechanics of the show’s plot devices as revealed in paratext such as the N64 game, or the impressions and opinions given by the cast and crew of the show, including the Japanese voice cast and animators, the American dub director and voice cast, and Anno himself. This is a textual analysis, i.e. based solely on what appears on screen. Part of reason I’m approaching this material in this way is to offer a full explanation of my interpretation of EoE in particular (doing the entire series would be vastly time consuming, and I have too much work to do right now, maybe another time) and to disprove the long-held assumption that the occult and Western religious symbolism and imagery in Evangelion is haphazard or chosen merely to “look cool”. What I’m not interested in is the mechanics of how the show’s metaphysics work, paratext, continuity, or anything that treats EoE as “mere simulation”. The End of Evangelion is a highly abstract and symbolic work, much more so than the series from which it springs. It is a mistake to dismiss the density of this film’s visual language, particularly given that this is animation- everything, every single thing we see was put there as a deliberate choice, every object was created. Such apparently minor things as a doorknob are works of art and not mere objects. With animation, we go beyond set design and art direction and see an entire visual language created whole cloth in front of our eyes. During this analysis I will touch on: The opening scene (aka That Scene) and its symbolic resonance with the Kitchen Scene and the Beach Scene, which are in fact the same thing repeated three times. The symbolism of hands and feet. Everybody knows about the hands, but the feet are important, too. The meaning of EoE’s occult language and symbolism, and its relationship with the chakras and the qabbalistic/kabbalistic sefirot. This part is fun? Did you know that Misato/Asuka/Rei, Asuka/Yui/Lilith, and the three Reis that appear behind Gendo after he is bitten in half all represent the tripartite goddess of the crossroads? It's true! Alternative interpretations of the film’s meaning, including End of Evangelion as a love story (A touching one, at that) End of Evangelion as an occult text (The pain of death is the joy of rebirth. Also, Rei is the goddes of light and knowledge Sophia and Kaworu is the demon Choronzon) The Dream Theory, which posits that Instrumentality began when Shinji saw the phantom Rei in Episode 1 The last of these in particular I feel is meaningful to discuss as the issue was raised in the now closed thread on That Scene that seeing the entire series as a dream somehow invalidates it or robs it of it’s meaning. (My posts in that thread begin here: http://forum.evageeks.org/post/604135/Masturbating-on-top-of-a-comatose-body/#604135) While I am about to begin with a discussion of the hospital scene, I’d prefer to avoid a long and tedious repeat of the argument about Shinji’s morality and whether he’s a wuss or not. I’m not here to discuss that. I’m more interested in proving he’s the beast of the apocalypse. Producing all of this for your entertainment and edification will be length and ongoing process. I have over 300 screenshots to weave into my analysis, some of which I had to take while skipping ahead one frame at a time as they appear very briefly yet are immensely meaningful to the narrative and the film’s system of symbols. On a technical note, I apologize in advance for the poor quality of my screencaps. I’m working from my own rip of the film from a Netflix DVD, so you can imagine how bad it’s going to look. Let’s get started by looking at the opening of the film. I’m starting with the hospital scene proper, as the actual opening is more germane to the dream interpretation. --- Part I: The Hospital Scene SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Wed Mar 06, 2013 8:58 pm Amo te; I look forward to the continuation.

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: A Textual Analysis             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Mar 06, 2013 9:45 pm Having done something similar myself some time ago, though not as comprehensibly, I find the idea of a personal commentary of EoE to be an interesting subject for a thread. Your position on the deeper significance of western religious symbolism might end up being controversial, but your analysis of imagery in general here is certainly tickling the odd analysis bone. I'm not sure if I'd agree with some conclusions, but I did enjoy reading the interpretations. Chuckman wrote: The opening scene (aka That Scene) and its symbolic resonance with the Kitchen Scene and the Beach Scene, which are in fact the same thing repeated three times.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Wed Mar 06, 2013 9:58 pm Alright, I do have something to note. The shot of the light- is it worth pointing out that the light is artificial? If the light is God or the soul, wouldn't it be more effective to use the actual sun, the symbol of divinity? We could have seen, for instance, light passing through the gaps in the shades. Instead we see the equivalent of the torches in Plato's cave- a weak imitation of the true sun. Perhaps it shows that Shinji is so fallen that he will not look even at the false light.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Mar 06, 2013 10:32 pm The true light is there, pouring in the windows. It's on the other side of Asuka's body, even. The light being artificial could also signify the theme of artificial divinity, gods made out of/into machines. Whether or not Asuka slashed her wrist or not isn't really that important. The image is there, and it's specifically her left wrist that's bandaged. Her right hand, her true self, is unharmed, and is the part she unconsciously extends to Shinji.

[]:             Quote Post by pioneer » Thu Mar 07, 2013 12:26 am It's amazing that you noticed the screws on the doorknob! I haven't watch the series in a while, but I watched 2.22 last night, and Misato explains that there's no locks because in Japan, respecting privacy is a virtue. And as we know, Shinji is not doing something virtuous. Also, I never noticed Asuka's breasts in the background of the shot of Shinji's hand. Ever. I've seen EoE countless times too! I guess because I'm gay, I'm more inclined to notice the semen covered hand rather than the tits lol

[]:             Quote Post by Minaplo » Thu Mar 07, 2013 6:49 am Chuckman wrote: I’ll be discussing the coffee thing later. Trust me, coffee in EoE represents the blood of Jesus.

[]:             Quote Post by Blue Monday » Thu Mar 07, 2013 9:16 am pioneer wrote: Misato explains that there's no locks because in Japan, respecting privacy is a virtue.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Fri Mar 08, 2013 2:57 am Blue Monday wrote: I've said it before but I agree in thinking the imagery wasn't just chosen because it looks cool (Yes, that may be a big part of it aesthetically, and the creators play it up in an almost self-deprecating way - I still tend to think there's more to it than that). I also like to have more fun with its meaning/reason in-narrative.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Mar 08, 2013 3:19 pm Really really love this thread. I might not agree with some of the interpretations or the idea that everything in the screen had an intentional significance by virtue of this being animation, but it's still very interesting and fresh to see such a thorought analysis on the movie (Really hope we see such an analysis of the series as well, someday) I must admit that the idea of coffee as blood of christ and the visual evidence you presented really left me shocked/awed, i have no idea how i didn't notice that. Your observation that someone took care of Asuka is also a pretty amazing and unique one, and i think that your analysis of EoE as a "love story" riddled with hope is the one i'll find most interesting. I am also among the people who feel that the religious imagery wasn't just chosen to "look cool" or anything like that. While most people who bring it up often approach it from the wrong angle, it seems that Chuckman's analysis is getting it right. Also, i'm not very sure what, but there is something that really catches my eye in that last picture you posted. I'm not very sure why, but it inspires a feeling of majestic beauty within me. Don't really know if there is any significance or symbolism behind it, but it certainly seems to be very aesthetically interesting. Just one thing though, if it was Shinji who attended to her (covered her and tidied her up, etc.) Wouldn't that somewhat undermine the idea that he was completely self-absorbed in that scene?

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: A Textual Analysis             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Fri Mar 08, 2013 5:07 pm ...

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: A Textual Analysis             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Mar 08, 2013 5:16 pm Seele00TextOnly wrote: We're never told explicitly that she's in a coma

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: A Textual Analysis             Quote Post by Sachi » Fri Mar 08, 2013 6:12 pm Reichu wrote: The whole incident is so much more gross when you remember that men in black follow Shinji around everywhere.

[]: Re: The End of Evangelion: A Textual Analysis             Quote Post by Dream » Fri Mar 08, 2013 11:22 pm Sachi wrote: On that note: there's an interesting lack of anything Intelligence Department-related after episode 21. I wonder what they were up to when Shinji was about to get shot in the head, or had all descended into chaos at that point?

I might not agree with some of the interpretations or the idea that everything in the screen had an intentional significance by virtue of this being animation, but it's still very interesting and fresh to see such a thorought analysis on the movie (Really hope we see such an analysis of the series as well, someday) I must admit that the idea of coffee as blood of christ and the visual evidence you presented really left me shocked/awed, i have no idea how i didn't notice that. Your observation that someone took care of Asuka is also a pretty amazing and unique one, and i think that your analysis of EoE as a "love story" riddled with hope is the one i'll find most interesting. Just one thing though, if it was Shinji who attended to her (covered her and tidied her up, etc.) Wouldn't that somewhat undermine the idea that he was completely self-absorbed in that scene?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Mar 09, 2013 8:57 pm Chuckman wrote: Asuka lying in the bed is a glass of water resting on a table, and some people will see it half empty while other see it half full, and some will see water in a glass resting on a table. These are all true, but the truth one person sees speaks to them.

Just one thing though, if it was Shinji who attended to her (covered her and tidied her up, etc.) Wouldn't that somewhat undermine the idea that he was completely self-absorbed in that scene?
